title: Type Casting
description: Implicit vs explicit conversion between types, static_cast, the older C-style cast, and why int/int division surprises beginners.
objectives: Perform explicit type casting with static_cast
Recognize the older C-style cast and why static_cast is preferred
Understand why dividing two ints truncates the result
Know when truncation happens vs when a value is preserved

sections.text: We saw in Data Types that assigning a double to an int truncates it: int x = 9.7; gives 9. That conversion between types is called casting. Sometimes it happens automatically, and sometimes we ask for it explicitly.
Type casting is converting a value from one data type to another, either automatically (implicit) or explicitly requested by the programmer.

sections.code:
int wholeNumber = 9.7;   // double → int, automatic
double price = 10;       // int → double, automatic

sections.text: The compiler converts values between types automatically when needed — this is implicit casting. No special syntax is required; it just happens.

rows: Safe — the whole number becomes a decimal with .0, nothing is lost
Lossy — the decimal part is truncated (dropped), not rounded

sections.text: Implicit casting is convenient, but a double → int conversion silently loses information. The compiler generally won't stop you — it's worth being deliberate about it.

static_cast<Type>(value) explicitly requests a conversion. It does exactly what the implicit conversion would have done here, but writing it out makes the intent obvious to anyone reading the code — this conversion is deliberate, not an accident.
static_cast<Type>(value) is C++'s standard way to explicitly convert a value to a different type.

(int)price is the older, C-style cast syntax, inherited from C. It still works in C++, and you'll see it in older code, but static_cast<int>(price) is generally preferred because it's more specific about what kind of conversion is happening and easier to search for in a codebase.

7 / 2 is mathematically 3.5, but with two int operands, C++ performs integer division — the result is truncated to an int, giving 3, not 3.5. Nothing was cast to double, so there was no decimal to keep.
Casting just one operand to double is enough — once either side of / is a double, C++ performs the division as double math, and the result keeps its decimal part.

sections.title: Pouring liquid into a smaller jar
sections.text: Casting a double into an int is like pouring a full jug of water into a smaller jar — whatever doesn't fit spills out and is gone. Casting an int into a double is the opposite: pouring a small amount into a much bigger jar, nothing is lost, there's just extra empty room (the .0).

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Expecting int / int to give a decimal result — it performs integer division and truncates, e.g. 7 / 2 gives 3.
Assuming static_cast<int>(x) rounds — it truncates, exactly like implicit double → int conversion.
Casting the wrong operand — static_cast<double>(a) / b works because it forces double math, but a / static_cast<double>(b) works too; casting neither still gives integer division.
Relying only on implicit casting for important conversions — an explicit static_cast makes the intent clear and easier to spot in review.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between implicit and explicit casting?
questions.answer: Implicit casting happens automatically when the compiler converts between types as needed; explicit casting is a deliberate conversion requested by the programmer, such as with static_cast.

questions.question: What does static_cast<int>(9.7) return?
questions.answer: 9 — the decimal part is truncated, not rounded.

questions.question: Why does 7 / 2 give 3 instead of 3.5 in C++?
questions.answer: Because both operands are int, C++ performs integer division, which truncates the result to a whole number rather than producing a decimal.

questions.question: How do you fix integer division to get a decimal result?
questions.answer: Cast at least one operand to a floating-point type, e.g. static_cast<double>(a) / b, so the division is performed using double arithmetic.

questions.question: Why is static_cast preferred over the C-style cast?
questions.answer: static_cast is more explicit about the intended conversion, is easier to search for in a codebase, and is generally considered safer and clearer in modern C++.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Type casting converts a value from one type to another. It can happen implicitly, as when a double is automatically assigned to an int and truncated, or explicitly, using static_cast<Type>(value) to make the conversion deliberate and visible. A common surprise is that dividing two ints performs integer division and truncates the result — 7 / 2 gives 3, not 3.5 — which can be fixed by casting one operand to double first. static_cast is generally preferred over the older C-style (Type)value cast because it's clearer about intent.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Type Casting
sections.text: double → int = pour into a smaller jar, decimals spill out. int → double = pour into a bigger jar, nothing lost. int / int = integer division, always truncates.
